name: Duke Parapsychology
description: Duke Parapsychology refers to the research and investigations into extrasensory perception (ESP) and psychokinesis (PK) conducted primarily at Duke University, most notably under the direction of J.B. Rhine and his wife, Louisa E. Rhine, from the 1930s to the 1960s. This research sought to establish the existence of paranormal phenomena through controlled laboratory experiments using statistical methods. Rhine developed card-guessing experiments (using Zener cards) and dice-throwing experiments to test ESP and PK, respectively. Proponents claim that the statistically significant results obtained in these experiments provide evidence for the existence of paranormal abilities. However, the methodology and interpretation of results have been heavily criticized by the scientific community. Critics argue that the experimental designs were flawed, lacked adequate controls, and that statistical analyses were often misinterpreted or subject to biases. The Rhine Research Center, which continues the work initiated at Duke, acknowledges the historical controversies but maintains its commitment to rigorous scientific investigation of paranormal phenomena. Mainstream science largely dismisses the claims of Duke Parapsychology, citing lack of replicability, methodological flaws, and the absence of a plausible mechanism for these phenomena to operate within known physics. The field's focus on statistical anomalies rather than identifying underlying causes has also drawn criticism.
subtitle: Pioneering, controversial ESP research at Duke.

claims.claim: ESP exists and can be measured statistically.
claims.evidence: Statistically significant results from Zener card experiments conducted at Duke.
claims.criticism: Flawed experimental design, lack of proper controls, and potential for sensory leakage or unconscious cuing.

claims.evidence: Statistically significant deviations from chance in dice-throwing experiments conducted at Duke.
claims.criticism: Small effect sizes, potential for unconscious influence by the experimenter, and difficulties in replicating results.

claims.claim: Paranormal abilities are innate human capacities.
claims.evidence: Consistent findings of ESP and PK across different participants in Duke's experiments.
claims.criticism: Lack of independent verification, potential for selection bias in participants, and difficulty controlling for confounding variables.

keyFigures.name: J.B. Rhine
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Parapsychology Laboratory at Duke University.

keyFigures.name: Louisa E. Rhine
keyFigures.role: Parapsychologist and researcher at Duke University, specializing in spontaneous ESP experiences.

keyFigures.name: C.E.M. Hansel
keyFigures.role: Psychologist and prominent critic of Rhine's methodology.

timeline.event: J.B. Rhine joins Duke University to study parapsychology.
Rhine begins conducting card-guessing and dice-throwing experiments.
Rhine publishes 'Extra-Sensory Perception', outlining his findings.
J.B. Rhine retires from Duke University.
The Rhine Research Center continues parapsychological research.
